en.extract:
The kiwikiu or Maui parrotbill (Pseudonestor xanthophrys) is a species of  Hawaiian honeycreeper endemic to the island of Maui in Hawaii. It can only be found in 50 square kilometres (19 sq mi) of mesic and wet forests at 1,200–2,150 metres (3,940–7,050 ft) on the windward slopes of Haleakalā. This species is critically endangered, with an estimated population in 2016 of 250-540 individuals, but more recent estimates of less than 150 individuals.  Fossil evidence indicates that the bird could at one time be seen in dry forests at elevations as low as 200–300 metres (660–980 ft), as well as on the island of Molokaʻi.


Description

The Maui parrotbill is one of the larger Hawaiian honeycreepers, measuring 14 centimetres (5.5 in) and with a mass of 20–25 grams (0.71–0.88 oz). The bird is yellow on the breast, cheeks, and belly, olive-green on the wings, crown, tail, and back, and has a bright yellow supercilium. The upper mandible of the bird's beak is hooked and dark gray, while the lower mandible is chisel-like and pale ivory. Males are longer-winged, larger-billed, and heavier than females.  Juveniles are gray-green above and light gray ventrally.


Song
Its call is a short "chip", which is similar to the Maui Nui ʻalauahio, chirped every three to five seconds. It song consists of "cheer" notes that are slower and richer than the ʻākepa. It also has a short song that sounds like "cheer-wee".


Diet
The Maui parrotbill is an insectivore.  It uses its large beak and powerful jaw muscles to remove bark and wood from small trees and shrubs such as ʻākala (Rubus hawaiensis), kanawao (Broussaisia arguta), and ʻōhiʻa lehua (Metrosideros polymorpha), eating the insects underneath. The Maui parrotbill also bites open fruits in search of insects. It is fond of moth pupae and beetle larvae.  Pairs of birds forage in a territory of 2.3 hectares (5.7 acres), which they must defend from competing parrotbills.


Breeding
The Maui parrotbill is monogamous and breeds between November and June.  Females build a cup-shaped nest out of Usnea lichens and pūkiawe (Styphelia tameiameiae) twigs, placing it 12 metres (39 ft) above the forest floor.  Pairs raise a single nestling per season.  The female incubates it for 16 days. Fledglings remain with their parents for five to eight months as they learn how to forage.


Habitat and distribution
The Maui parrotbill currently lives only in undisturbed wet forests dominated by ʻōhiʻa lehua (Metrosideros polymorpha) and small patches of ʻōhiʻa-koa (Acacia koa) mesic forest.  Its habitat exhibits a dense understory of small trees, shrubs, epiphytes, ferns, and sedges, centered between Puʻu ʻAlaea, Kuhiwa Valley, Lake Waianapanapa, and upper Kīpahulu Valley, an area less than 2,020 hectares (5,000 acres), at elevations of 1,310–2,070 metres (4,300–6,790 ft).


Hawaiian name
As far as anyone can determine, Pseudonestor xanthophrys had not historically had a common name in the Hawaiian language.  The Hawaiian name kiwikiu was developed by the Hawaiian Lexicon Committee, who was contacted by the Maui Forest Bird Recovery Project to select an appropriate name.  A naming ceremony was held in the bird's habitat in September 2010.  The "kiwi" part of the name means bent or curved (e.g., sickle-shaped), which refers to the shape of the bill of this bird.  "Kiu" has a double meaning, referring both to the bird's secretive ways and to a cold, chilly wind, such as the breezes in the bird's habitat.


Conservation
The Maui parrotbill's natural habitat is mesic and wet forests. It is threatened by habitat loss. Much of the land in the parrotbill's historic range was changed for agricultural purposes, timber production and animal grazing. Introduced pests, such as mosquitoes, rats, and feral ungulates directly and indirectly affect the parrotbill's survival. Mosquitoes spread avian malaria, which the parrotbill is susceptible to, rats prey upon the birds' eggs and young, and feral pigs uproot the low-lying vegetation that the parrotbill forages in. Pigs additionally create wallows, which serve as breeding grounds for avian malaria-infected mosquitoes.
The Maui parrotbill was listed as an endangered species in 1967 under the Endangered Species Preservation Act. It is also part of the Maui-Molokai Bird Recovery Plan in 1984, which led to fencing areas of East Maui and removing feral ungulates. The recovery plan also included a captive breeding program, which produced its first chick in 2003.  Field research is primarily done by the Maui Forest Bird Recovery Project.
A 2009 survey of the Waikamoi Preserve estimated that there were about 20 birds per square kilometer in the windward preserve near the summit of Haleakalā, indicating that the population was holding steady or possibly increasing. A contributing factor is that native shrub cover in Waikamoi has tripled in the past 15 years. A previous study found about half the density. The preserve contains about 25 percent of the population, while most of the rest is in the Hanawi Natural Area Reserve. The birds were once found throughout Maui and Molokai. A 2019 effort is underway to reintroduce Maui parrotbill to leeward Maui, where it had been previously extirpated. However, more recent studies have found that the population in fact dramatically declined after 2001, as climate change has made more of the higher-elevation forests that the kiwikiu inhabits more hospitable to mosquitoes. A translocation effort to a restored area on the leeward slopes of Haleakalā in 2019 failed after most birds succumbed to avian malaria, despite otherwise doing well in the habitat. 
It has been predicted that if the mosquito population continues increasing, Maui parrotbills may face functional extinction in the wild by 2027. Due to this, there have been several conservation efforts planned by the Maui Forest Bird Working Group, including at least temporarily fostering a sizeable captive population on zoos in the mainland United States (previous ex-situ conservation efforts were limited to only Hawaii), including potentially the National Aviary in Pennsylvania, Smithsonian Conservation Biology Institute in Virginia and the Tracy Aviary in Utah, potentially introduce a wild population to the island of Hawaii as a backup, manage and restore more habitat in Maui for eventual release, develop predator control tools, and perform landscape-scale mosquito control in the bird's native habitat. The captive population will consist of 15 male and 15 female wild birds; removing such a large number of birds from an already-small wild population may accelerate the timeline of the species' possible extinction in the wild, but it can allow for a safe population to be bred in captivity away from avian malaria.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet.
Hilford, Jason (April 2006). "Hatching a Plan". Maui Nō Ka ʻOi Magazine. 10 (2).
Maui Parrotbill videos, photos and sounds on the Internet Bird Collection

The '''kiwikiu''' or '''Maui parrotbill''' ('''''Pseudonestor xanthophrys''''') is a species of  [[Hawaiian honeycreeper]] [[Endemism|endemic]] to the island of [[Maui]] in [[Hawaii]]. It can only be found in {{convert|50|km2|sqmi}} of [[Hawaiian tropical rainforests#Mixed mesic forests|mesic]] and [[Hawaiian tropical rainforests#Wet forests|wet forests]] at {{convert|1200|-|2150|m|ft}} on the windward slopes of [[Haleakalā]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.state.hi.us/dlnr/dofaw/cwcs/files/NAAT%20final%20CWCS/Chapters/Terrestrial%20Fact%20Sheets/Forest%20Birds/Maui%20Parrotbill%20NAAT%20final%20!.pdf |title=Maui Parrotbill |work=Hawaii's Comprehensive Wildlife Conservation Strategy |publisher=State of Hawaiʻi |date=2005-10-01 |access-date=2009-03-22}}</ref> This species is [[Critically Endangered|critically endangered]], with an estimated population in 2016 of 250-540 individuals, but more recent estimates of less than 150 individuals.<ref name="BirdLife">{{cite web |url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=8905&m=0 |title=Maui Parrotbill (''Pseudonestor xanthophrys'')|work=BirdLife Species Factsheets |publisher=[[BirdLife International]] |access-date=2009-03-22}}</ref>  Fossil evidence indicates that the bird could at one time be seen in [[Hawaiian tropical dry forests|dry forests]] at elevations as low as {{convert|200|-|300|m}}, as well as on the island of [[Molokai|Moloka{{okina}}i]].<ref name="FWS">{{Cite web |title=Revised Recovery Plan for Hawaiian Forest Birds |url=https://fws.gov/pacific/ecoservices/endangered/recovery/documents/HawaiianForestBirdsRevRP2006PartII.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170125042341/https://fws.gov/pacific/ecoservices/endangered/recovery/documents/HawaiianForestBirdsRevRP2006PartII.pdf |archive-date=January 25, 2017 |access-date=May 8, 2023}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Pseudonestor xanthophrys AvesHawaiienses00Wils 0190.jpg|thumb|Illustration by [[Frederick William Frohawk]]]]
The Maui parrotbill is one of the larger [[Hawaiian honeycreeper]]s, measuring {{convert|14|cm|in}}<ref name="BirdLife"/> and with a mass of {{convert|20|-|25|g|oz}}. The bird is yellow on the breast, cheeks, and belly, olive-green on the wings, crown, tail, and back, and has a bright yellow [[supercilium]]. The upper [[mandible]] of the bird's [[beak]] is hooked and dark gray, while the lower mandible is chisel-like and pale ivory. Males are longer-winged, larger-billed, and heavier than females.  Juveniles are gray-green above and light gray ventrally.<ref name="FWS"/>

===Song===
Its call is a short "chip", which is similar to the [[Maui Nui ʻalauahio|Maui Nui {{okina}}alauahio]], chirped every three to five seconds. It song consists of "cheer" notes that are slower and richer than the [[ʻAkepa|{{okina}}ākepa]]. It also has a short song that sounds like "cheer-wee".

== Diet ==
The Maui parrotbill is an [[insectivore]].  It uses its large beak and powerful jaw muscles to remove [[Bark (botany)|bark]] and wood from small [[tree]]s and [[shrub]]s such as ''{{okina}}ākala'' (''[[Rubus hawaiensis]]''), ''kanawao'' (''[[Broussaisia arguta]]''), and ''{{okina}}ōhi{{okina}}a lehua'' (''[[Metrosideros polymorpha]]''), eating the [[insect]]s underneath. The Maui parrotbill also bites open [[fruit]]s in search of insects. It is fond of [[moth]] pupae and [[beetle]] [[larvae]].  Pairs of birds forage in a territory of {{convert|2.3|ha|acre}}, which they must defend from competing parrotbills.<ref name="FWS"/>

== Breeding ==
The Maui parrotbill is [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] and breeds between November and June.  Females build a cup-shaped nest out of ''[[Usnea]]'' [[lichen]]s and ''pūkiawe'' (''[[Styphelia]] tameiameiae'') twigs, placing it {{convert|12|m|ft}} above the forest floor.  Pairs raise a single nestling per season.  The female incubates it for 16 days. Fledglings remain with their parents for five to eight months as they learn how to forage.<ref name="FWS"/>

== Habitat and distribution ==
The Maui parrotbill currently lives only in undisturbed [[Hawaiian tropical rainforests#Wet forests|wet forests]] dominated by ''{{okina}}ōhi{{okina}}a lehua'' (''[[Metrosideros polymorpha]]'') and small patches of ''{{okina}}ōhi{{okina}}a''-''koa'' (''[[Acacia koa]]'') [[Hawaiian tropical rainforests#Mixed mesic forests|mesic forest]].  Its habitat exhibits a dense understory of small [[tree]]s, [[shrub]]s, [[epiphyte]]s, [[fern]]s, and [[Cyperaceae|sedge]]s,<ref name="FWS"/> centered between Pu{{okina}}u {{okina}}Alaea, Kuhiwa Valley, [[Wai'anapanapa State Park|Lake Waianapanapa]], and upper [[Kipahulu|Kīpahulu Valley]], an area less than {{convert|2020|ha|acre}}, at elevations of {{convert|1310|-|2070|m|ft}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|year=2000|title=Maui Parrotbill (Pseudonestor xanthophrys)|encyclopedia=Beacham's Guide to the Endangered Species of North America|publisher=[[Gale (Cengage)|Gale]]|location=[[Detroit]]|editor=Walton Beacham|volume=1|pages=505–506|editor2=Frank V. Castronova|editor3=Suzanne Sessine}}</ref>

==Hawaiian name==
As far as anyone can determine, ''Pseudonestor xanthophrys'' had not historically had a common name in the Hawaiian language.  The Hawaiian name ''kiwikiu'' was developed by the Hawaiian Lexicon Committee, who was contacted by the Maui Forest Bird Recovery Project to select an appropriate name.  A naming ceremony was held in the bird's habitat in September 2010.  The "kiwi" part of the name means bent or curved (e.g., sickle-shaped), which refers to the shape of the bill of this bird.  "Kiu" has a double meaning, referring both to the bird's secretive ways and to a cold, chilly wind, such as the breezes in the bird's habitat.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.mauinews.com/page/content.detail/id/531738.html |title=Parrotbill's got Hawaiian name |newspaper=The Maui News |date=2010-05-21 |access-date=2010-09-13}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.mauinews.com/page/content.detail/id/540256/Endangered-bird-to-receive-Hawaiian-name.html?nav=10 |title=Endangered bird to receive Hawaiian name |newspaper=The Maui News |date=2010-09-10 |access-date=2010-09-13}}</ref>

==Conservation==

The Maui parrotbill's natural [[habitat]] is [[Hawaiian tropical rainforests#Mixed mesic forests|mesic]] and [[Hawaiian tropical rainforests#Wet forests|wet forests]]. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]]. Much of the land in the parrotbill's historic range was changed for agricultural purposes, timber production and animal grazing. Introduced pests, such as mosquitoes, rats, and feral ungulates directly and indirectly affect the parrotbill's survival. Mosquitoes spread [[avian malaria]], which the parrotbill is susceptible to, rats prey upon the birds' eggs and young, and feral pigs uproot the low-lying vegetation that the parrotbill forages in. Pigs additionally create wallows, which serve as breeding grounds for avian malaria-infected mosquitoes.

The Maui parrotbill was listed as an endangered species in 1967 under the [[Endangered Species Preservation Act]]. It is also part of the Maui-Molokai Bird Recovery Plan in 1984, which led to fencing areas of East Maui and removing feral ungulates. The recovery plan also included a captive breeding program, which produced its first chick in 2003.  Field research is primarily done by the Maui Forest Bird Recovery Project.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Maui Forest Bird Recovery Project |url=https://www.mauiforestbirds.org/ |access-date=2023-05-09 |website=Maui Forest Bird Recovery Project |language=en-US}}</ref>

A 2009 survey of the Waikamoi Preserve estimated that there were about 20 birds per square kilometer in the windward preserve near the summit of Haleakalā, indicating that the population was holding steady or possibly increasing. A contributing factor is that native shrub cover in Waikamoi has tripled in the past 15 years. A previous study found about half the density. The preserve contains about 25 percent of the population, while most of the rest is in the Hanawi Natural Area Reserve. The birds were once found throughout Maui and Molokai. A 2019 effort is underway to reintroduce Maui parrotbill to leeward Maui, where it had been previously extirpated.<ref>
{{cite news |author=Hamilton |first=Chris |date=19 December 2009 |title=Study shows Maui parrotbill population is doing well |newspaper=Maui News |url=http://www.mauinews.com/page/content.detail/id/526862.html?nav=10 |access-date=19 December 2009}}</ref> However, more recent studies have found that the population in fact dramatically declined after 2001, as [[climate change]] has made more of the higher-elevation forests that the kiwikiu inhabits more hospitable to mosquitoes. A translocation effort to a restored area on the [[Windward and leeward|leeward]] slopes of [[Haleakalā]] in 2019 failed after most birds succumbed to avian malaria, despite otherwise doing well in the habitat.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|last=Cerizo|first=Kehaulani|date=March 27, 2022|title=Maui's dwindling kiwikiu to move to Mainland|url=https://www.mauinews.com/news/local-news/2021/03/mauis-dwindling-kiwikiu-to-move-to-mainland/|access-date=2021-04-18|website=The Maui News|language=en-US}}</ref> 

It has been predicted that if the mosquito population continues increasing, Maui parrotbills may face [[functional extinction]] [[Extinct in the wild|in the wild]] by 2027. Due to this, there have been several conservation efforts planned by the Maui Forest Bird Working Group, including at least temporarily fostering a sizeable captive population on zoos in the [[Contiguous United States|mainland United States]] (previous ex-situ conservation efforts were limited to only Hawaii), including potentially the [[National Aviary]] in [[Pennsylvania]], [[Smithsonian Conservation Biology Institute]] in [[Virginia]] and the [[Liberty Park (Salt Lake City)|Tracy Aviary]] in [[Utah]], potentially introduce a wild population to the [[Hawaii (island)|island of Hawaii]] as a backup, manage and restore more habitat in Maui for eventual release, develop predator control tools, and perform landscape-scale [[mosquito control]] in the bird's native habitat.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Truesdale|first=Jack|date=2021-04-27|title=Maui Is No Longer Safe For Hawaii's Endangered Birds, Biologists Say|url=https://www.civilbeat.org/2021/04/maui-is-no-longer-safe-for-hawaiis-endangered-birds-biologists-say/|access-date=2021-04-28|website=Honolulu Civil Beat|language=en}}</ref> The captive population will consist of 15 male and 15 female wild birds; removing such a large number of birds from an already-small wild population may accelerate the timeline of the species' possible extinction in the wild, but it can allow for a safe population to be bred in captivity away from avian malaria.<ref name=":0" />
